coredata.dc:description: Non-invasive and high spatiotemporal resolution mythologies for the diagnosis and treatment of disease in clinical medicine promote the development of modern medicine. Ultrasound (US) technology provides a non-invasive, real-time, and cost-effective clinical imaging modality, which plays a significant role in chemical synthesis and clinical translation, especially in in vivo imaging and cancer therapy. On the one hand, the US treatment is usually accompanied by cavitation, leading to high temperature and pressure, so-called “hot spot”, playing a significant role in sonochemical-based colloidal synthesis. Compared with the classical nucleation synthetic method, the sonochemical synthesis strategy presents high efficiency for the fabrication of colloidal nanocrystals due to its fast nucleation and growth procedure. On the other hand, the US is attractive for in vivo and medical treatment, with applications increasing with the development of novel contrast agents, such as the micro and nano bubbles, which are widely used in neuromodulation, with which the US can breach the blood–brain barrier temporarily and safely, opening a new door to neuromodulation and therapy. In terms of cancer treatment, sonodynamic therapy and US-assisted synergetic therapy show great effects against cancer and sonodynamic immunotherapy present unparalleled potentiality compared with other synergetic therapies. Further development of ultrasound technology can revolutionize both chemical synthesis and clinical translation by improving efficiency, precision, and accessibility while reducing environmental impact and enhancing patient care. In this paper, we review the US-assisted sonochemical synthesis and biological applications, to promote the next generation US technology-assisted applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Non-invasive and high spatiotemporal resolution mythologies for the diagnosis and treatment of disease in clinical medicine promote the development of modern medicine. Ultrasound (US) technology provides a non-invasive, real-time, and cost-effective clinical imaging modality, which plays a significant role in chemical synthesis and clinical translation, especially in in vivo imaging and cancer therapy. On the one hand, the US treatment is usually accompanied by cavitation, leading to high temperature and pressure, so-called “hot spot”, playing a significant role in sonochemical-based colloidal synthesis. Compared with the classical nucleation synthetic method, the sonochemical synthesis strategy presents high efficiency for the fabrication of colloidal nanocrystals due to its fast nucleation and growth procedure. On the other hand, the US is attractive for in vivo and medical treatment, with applications increasing with the development of novel contrast agents, such as the micro and nano bubbles, which are widely used in neuromodulation, with which the US can breach the blood–brain barrier temporarily and safely, opening a new door to neuromodulation and therapy. In terms of cancer treatment, sonodynamic therapy and US-assisted synergetic therapy show great effects against cancer and sonodynamic immunotherapy present unparalleled potentiality compared with other synergetic therapies. Further development of ultrasound technology can revolutionize both chemical synthesis and clinical translation by improving efficiency, precision, and accessibility while reducing environmental impact and enhancing patient care. In this paper, we review the US-assisted sonochemical synthesis and biological applications, to promote the next generation US technology-assisted applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultrasound (US) technology, although it has existed for years, is becoming more sophisticated and attractive to fields as diverse as military radar to state of the art clinical medical applications associating nanoscience and technology [1–4]. Up to now, US technology plays a significant role in chemical synthesis and clinical translation. Benefiting from generation of “hot spot” during the ultrasonic process, sonochemistry has a faster reaction speed and higher reaction efficiency, facilitating various shapes of colloidal nanocrystals. The contribution of US technology in clinical translation is reflected in the imaging and therapy of diseases. With the development of technology, there are many kinds of clinical imaging methods, however, they all have shortcomings that cannot be ignored. For example, fluorescence imaging (FI) is limited to penetration depth and dispersion, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is time-consuming and positron emission tomography-computed tomography (PET-CT) is expensive. Compared to traditional imaging methods, US technology assisted ultrasonography (USG) is by far the most frequently used noninvasive, real-time, and convenient medical imaging method for the diagnosis and treatment of clinical diseases [5–9]. Spatial resolution refers to the minimum limit of the spatial geometric size of recognizable adjacent objects in an image, reflecting imaging quality. Tissue penetration represents the maximum depth of valuable signal in the body. Benefiting from the unique frequency of ultrasound, USG is provided with high spatial resolution and depth of tissue penetration [10–12] (the maximum penetration depth is up to 15 mm), utilized for routine obstetric examinations and certain preclinical studies.

Advances in US technology have further promoted its therapeutic applications. These include the widely reported focused ultrasound (FUS) and sonodynamic therapy (SDT)[13–18]. FUS uses the focus capabilities and high penetration of ultrasound to pinpoint precise targets under the guidance of imaging, which also involves energy conversion equipment that converts the mechanical effect of ultrasonic waves into a thermal melting effect, so that the target cells coagulate at necrosis, while the tiny bubbles generated by cavitation effect collapse. These produce high temperature and high pressure that destroy target cells but do not affect the surrounding normal tissues, so that the procedure remains non-invasive [19]. The advantages of FUS are that the operation is simple and non-invasive without bleeding, and brings less impact on life. The tumor dissolved in the body is digested outside the body, which can reduce the pain of treatment and the cost is lower than that of surgical. Unlike the sono-thermal conversion of FUS, sonodynamic therapy uses US activation of a sonosensitizer to produce reactive oxygen species which converts US energy into chemical energy to induce target cell death through apoptosis and/or necrosis [20]. In recent years, researchers have found that the ability of SDT to induce tumor fragments to trigger host anti-tumor immune effects can be combined with immunotherapy, which can effectively inhibit tumor growth while stimulating systemic anti-cancer immunity, inhibiting tumor metastasis, provide long-term immune memory effects, and hence contribute to preventing tumor recurrence [21–23]. Like FUS, SDT is non-invasive and safe. What’s more, benefiting from sonosensitizer, SDT shows higher accuracy and targeting and has the potential to be synergistic with other therapeutic approaches. However, FUS and SDT also have many aspects to be improved. For example, FUS is suitable for treating tumors less than 2 mm in diameter, but is less effective in the face of large tumors. The therapeutic effect of single SDT is not very significant and usually plays a significant contribution in the field of synergistic therapy.

This review summarizes recent developments in US-based colloidal synthesis and biological applications (Fig. 1 ). Section 2 briefly introduces the history of ultrasound technology. It then summarizes current ultrasound synthesis methods of colloidal nanocrystals. The following sections examine the applications of ultrasound technology to in vivo imaging and cancer treatment. The discussion centers on current challenges and the potential of these new ultrasound applications. The overarching aim is to stimulate researchers’ enthusiasm to further the development of ultrasonic chemistry in synthesis and biological applications. Advancing ultrasound technology in chemistry and biology offers benefits such as faster reactions, environmental friendliness, and precise control. In the medical field, it enhances non-invasive imaging, real-time monitoring, and innovative therapies, improving patient care. Portable solutions expand access to diagnostics, and multimodal integration enhances diagnostic accuracy. Overall, ultrasound's development promises efficiency, sustainability, and improved healthcare.

Ultrasound technology was first developed in 1880, when the Curie brothers discovered that applying pressure or tension in a specific direction of a quartz crystal causes an electric charge to appear on its surface, and that the density of the charge was proportional to the magnitude of the applied external force. Today this is known as the positive piezoelectric effect. Soon after, they identified the inverse piezoelectric effect, and reported the forward and reverse piezoelectric coefficient of quartz crystals in 1881. The piezoelectric effect makes it possible to use electronic technology to create US, which paved the way for the development of US technology. After hundreds of years of research, US technology developed greatly. The effects of US technology, including mechanical effect [24], cavitation effect [25,26], thermal effect [27,28], acoustic effect [29], chemical effect [30] and biological effect [31] are widely applied in industry, medical and other fields. Sonochemistry, an emerging discipline the cross-penetration of acoustics and chemistry, lies on the frontier of acoustics and chemistry. It uses ultrasonic waves to accelerate chemical reactions, improve chemical yields, change reaction processes, improve reaction conditions and trigger new chemical reactions at times.

The actual discovery of sonochemistry dates back to 1895, when Thornycroft and Barnaby discovered that diving propellers submerged in water were severely eroded, and that cavitation bubbles formed on high-speed rotating propellers. This was also the first paper in the world on cavitation. In 1917, Rayleigh introduced the initial mathematical model depicting cavitation within an incompressible liquid [32]. The revelation of ultrasound's capacity to accelerate reaction rates due to its chemical and biological properties was first documented by Alfred L. Loomis in 1927. Subsequently, in 1935, Frenzel and Schule made the pioneering observation that intense ultrasound exposure led to light emission in water [33].

In 1944, Weiss noted that subjecting water to sonication resulted in the production of hydrogen and hydroxyl radicals [34]. The computational simulation of the cavitation process within bubbles was achieved for the first time in 1950 [35]. It wasn't until 1980 that Neppiras introduced the term “sonochemistry” while discussing acoustic cavitation in a review [36]. In April 1986, the first International Symposium on Biochemistry was held at the University of Warwick, England, marking the birth of the emerging discipline of sonochemistry. In 1994, the first issue of the academic journal of sonochemistry, Ultrasonics Sonochemistry, was published. Currently sonochemistry technology has a wide range of applications in organic synthesis [37–40], materials [41–45], organometallic chemistry [46,47] and industrial manufacturing [48,49].

The frequency of US is generally between 20 KHz and 10 MHz and US in the liquid cause acoustic cavitation, which is the foundation of sonochemical synthesis. Sonochemical synthesis has three phases:I. Nucleation. US causes local tensile stress in the liquid, resulting in a decrease in the internal pressure of the liquid, so that exerting negative pressure. The continuously increasing negative pressure will break down the intermolecular van der Waals forces of the liquid, giving rise to that the gas originally dissolved in the liquid is supersaturated and overflows from the liquid forming small bubbles. The strong tensile stress “tears” the liquid to form a cavity at the same time composed of vapor or another gas dissolved in the liquid until the liquid medium forms the tiny bubble nuclei [50]; II. Growth. When the ultrasonic energy is high enough, the tiny bubble (cavitation nucleus) in the liquid vibrates, grows and continuously gathers the sound field energy under the action of the ultrasonic field; III. Implosion collapse. When the energy reaches a certain threshold, the cavitation bubble can no longer absorb energy efficiently, in the phase of which, they collapse and close in sharply due to the insufficiency of energy input. Ultrasonic cavitation (Fig. 2 ) concentrates sound field energy, and then releases it in a very small space at a very short time after the cavitation bubble collapses. This results in extremely high temperature and pressure that is difficult to produce in a liquid environment at normal temperature and pressure. This forms the so-called “hot spot”, which can reach a temperature of more than 5 000 K and a pressure of more than 1000 atmospheres, at a heating and cooling rate of more than 1010 K s−1, which can greatly increase the rate of chemical reactions, and at time enable chemical reactions that can trigger new chemical reactions [51–53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Traditional colloidal nanocrystal synthesis versus sonochemical synthesis

ce:section.ce:para: Classical nucleation theory posits that nuclei in homogeneous solution were the result of small and incompletely solvated nuclei exhibiting greater instability than solvated atoms/ions that subsequently formed large nanoparticles (Fig. 2a) [54]. Initially, individual species collide randomly in solution to form clusters, but these clusters are too small and unstable to cross the nucleation barrier, so that they dissolve into the solution to reform monomers. Over time, the supersaturation reaches a critical point, when the degree of random collision is so intense that the energy of the cluster is enough to cross the nucleation barrier, and its growth exceeds the critical size, thus forming a stable nucleation (nucleation stage) and begins to consume the raw growth material. After maintaining a short nucleation phase, the supersaturation of the solution drops below threshold and new nuclei no longer formed. Thereafter, the monomer will tend to continue growing on a stable nucleus, as long as the solution is supersaturated [56–58]. Temperature is a particularly crucial variable throughout the nucleation growth process, because it not only affects the saturation of the growth material and reaction balance, but also affects the reaction rate. The “thermal injection” synthesis of quantum dots is a good example, where reactants are injected at room temperature, resulting in a significant drop in temperature, a rapid drop in supersaturation below critical levels, and subsequent growth dominated by heterogeneous nucleation with lower energy requirements. This method of separating the nucleation and nucleation growth processes from the burst nucleation process results in a combination of fast nucleation and the ripening step [59,60].

US-assisted colloidal nanocrystal synthesis differs from traditional nucleation and growth mechanisms in several ways (Fig. 2b). Specifically, sonochemical synthesis is divided into three phases: nucleation, growth, and implosion collapse. Compared to the classical colloidal synthesis strategy, the direct heating process upon the precursor is replaced by the indirect US waves energy transfer process. Specifically, when the US waves meet the precursor, cyclic alternating areas of compression (high pressure) and rarefaction (low pressure) are formed. Then the nucleation of sonochemical synthesis begins with the generation of' microbubbles, which is attributed to decrease in the tensile strength of the liquid due to molecular clumps and impurities in the solution under low pressure. Nucleation of sonochemical synthesis begins with the generation of microbubbles, which is attributed to a decrease in the tensile strength of the liquid due to molecular clumps and impurities in the solution under the low pressure. Nucleation during synthesis can also be promoted by introducing US contrast agents containing exogenous microvesicles [61]. After nucleation, the oscillatory boost of US waves leads to nonlinear growth in bubble size, which grows in the negative sound pressure phase and shrinks in the positive sound pressure phase that is the process of bubble growth [50,55]. The ultrasonic energy is stored in the bubble during the bubble growth process. When the bubble reaches the critical size threshold, the bubble can no longer absorb energy and grow, becoming unstable. That means implodes and collapses, resulting in a microjet on the surface of the bubble, free radical formation, asymmetric collapse, shock waves, etc. Asymmetric collapse leads to extreme heat, extreme pressure, and the formation of sonoluminescence. Ultrasonic waves can also indirectly trigger chemical changes where the implosion collapse produces reactive free radicals, such as singlet oxygen and hydroxyl radicals, which in turn trigger subsequent chemical reactions which do not require the use of sonosensitizers. Findings indicate that under the action of US, water vapor can decompose in an aqueous solution to produce hydroxyl radicals and hydrogen radicals [62]. Compared to traditional colloidal nanocrystal synthesis strategies, sonochemical synthesis has a faster reaction speed, higher reaction efficiency, and milder reaction conditions because it can release high pressure and high heat in a very small space, and can synthesize various shapes of colloidal nanocrystals. S.Wu et al. report the novel crystal structure of three-dimensional {[Cu(en)2][KFe(CN)6]}n using [Cu(en)2]2+ as a template and K+ as a connecting unit. The SEM images show nanorods obtained from the direct reaction of two precursors solutions are irregular and without uniform size. By adding PVP into the K3[Fe(CN)6] aqueous solution under the reaction of US, the morphologies of the nanocrystals can been changed from nanorods to microcubes. The result indicates the remarkable effect of PVP on the crystallization of nanocrystals. It seems that PVP and ultrasonic radiation played a key role in the morphology development of the nano/micro-crystas [63]. The drawback is that the fast reaction speed makes it difficult to control the process of sonochemical synthesis, as discussed in detail below.

In this review, we focused on three typical colloidal nanocrystals based on the sonochemical synthesis: I, metal (Fig. 3 a) or alloy nanoparticles (NPs); II, metal oxides NPs (Fig. 3b-d); III, metal chalcogenides NPs (Fig. 3e-f). While unable to produce perfectly monodispersed metal nanoparticles, sonochemical synthesis contributes to the mass production of nanoparticles with enough uniformity. As depicted in Fig. 3a, a simple sonochemical co-reduction method has been reported for synthesizing Au-Ag core -shell nanoparticles and the methodology applies to the synthesis of bimetallic systems involving other metals with core–shell morpholohy [64]. Kenneth S. Suslick et al. discover a simple synthetic method to produce nanosized iron colloid using high-intensity US, which presents narrow size distributions compared with commercial magnetic fluids [65]. This advantage is easily extended to other metal oxide, such as TiO2 prepared by Chen et al. and CuO reported by Xie et al. [66,67]. So as is metal chalcogenide and it is easy to be stripped under the US due to its higher energy than metal oxide [68]. Alok Singh reports the single step sonochemical synthesis of copper sulphide nanoparticles under various reaction conditions, reducing the reaction time greatly compared with liquid phase synthesis at low temperature and reaching to higher reaction efficiency compared with solvothermal synthesis [69].

Generally, sonochemical colloidal nanocrystal synthesis presents great superiority in the synthesis of metal chalcogenides compared with traditional colloidal nanocrystal synthesis due to their high energy, but is inferior in metal or alloy nanoparticles because it has trouble in precise control of size in metal or alloy nanoparticles at present.

US is a safe, non-invasive, real-time, and cost-effective clinical imaging modality that can be applied to image tissues deep within the body. It is one of the most widely used imaging modalities for the detection and diagnosis of disease, with applications including fetal [70,71], genito-urinal [72,73], cardiovascular [74–76], orthopaedic [77,78], breast [79–81], and abdominal imaging [82]. US is also applied as a screening tool for early cancer detection due to its ability to differentiate cysts from solid masses based on target size, shape, and relative echogenicity [83,84]. It offers several advantages that make it attractive for in vivo imaging in medical applications: 1. Hazardless Radiation: The human body can absorb this harmless energy, which allows repeated examinations of the same patient with no risk, using standard equipment. 2. High Sampling Rate: Since the speed of sound in soft tissues is ∼ 1540 m/sec, and the typical ranges used in medical examinations are short (1 mm-25 cm), the time it takes for a wave to cover these ranges is only 10–1000 µsec. Numerous waves can be transmitted instantly to produce sufficient information to follow dynamic changes occurring inside the body [85–87]. 3. Compact Transducers: Most ultrasonic transducers used in medicine are made of very compact piezoelectric crystals (5–15 cm in length), although even smaller crystals can be manufactured, and a single transducer can be used to both transmit and receive waves. These miniature crystals are sufficiently small to be placed atop minimally invasive surgery devices, atop catheters, or implanted within the body, and thus can probe almost any region of the body [88–90]. 4. Numerous parameters can be measured simultaneously: Since the properties of acoustic waves are strongly related to the properties of the medium in which they travel, there is a wide variety of parameters that can be measured, such as muscle contractions, bone structure, blood vessel shapes and angles, and others [91,92]. 5. A cost effective technique: US does not require radio frequency shielded rooms. The transducers do not wear out and there is no need for films or expensive and dangerous materials [93]. US transducers are compact and versatile, making them suitable for various applications and allowing for easy and cost effective maneuverability during imaging procedures. US uses sound waves, making it a safe imaging modality without the harmful effects of ionizing radiation, such as X-rays. This is particularly important for pediatric and pregnant patients. In addition, US provides real-time imaging with a high sampling rate, allowing for dynamic visualization of moving structures like the beating heart or blood flow in vessels. Moreover, it provides real-time guidance during procedures like biopsies, injections, and surgeries, enhancing precision and reducing the risk of complications. In many cases, US imaging doesn't require the use of contrast agents, reducing the potential for allergic reactions or kidney-related complications. US can be used to assess various body systems, including abdominal [94], cardiac [95], vascular [96], musculoskeletal [97], and obstetric [98], making it a versatile tool in clinical practice. The components are relatively cheap. Thus, US is an excellent choice from the economical point of view.

These advantages help explain why the US technique is widely used in clinical applications around the world. US is used extensively in medicine, and new applications are frequently suggested. Currently, its main application is in the imaging of soft tissues, which provides a rapid and convenient tool for size and distance measurements of different organs and diseases, such as chronic liver disease, breast lesions, thyroid nodules, lymph node malignancies, and inflammatory bowel diseases. US techniques are also utilized in cardiovascular diagnosis. Based on the Doppler effect (the wavelength of the object's radiation varies because of the relative motion of the source and the observer), blood flow in various vessels and the valves of the heart can be imaged. Duplex imaging increasing the solution of a single exposure with two exposures allows the physician to combine an anatomical image with a color-coded flow or motion map.

Not all organs can be imaged noninvasively, such as cardiovascular disease, which is called “the first killer of human health” While non-invasive imaging modalities such as computed tomography angiography (CTA), positron emission tomography (PET) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can assess the morphological features and metabolic activity of atherosclerotic plaques, it is insufficient to resist cardiovascular disease, so invasive imaging techniques with increased resolution and precise visualization of the vessel and lumen dimensions are needed [99]. Several intrusive techniques have been developed for within body probes. The commonly used invasive imaging technique is Intravascular ultrasound (IVUS), the gold standard for coronary assessment [100]. IVUS is a catheter-based technique that provides 2D cross-sectional images of the arterial wall. The images are produced through a miniaturized transducer element located on the catheter tip. The images provide information on the luminal cross-sectional area, wall thickness, and wall structure. IVUS technology uses US with a center frequency of 20–45 MHz, but the resulting imaging spatial resolution makes it difficult to present the thin cap or microvessels, which is crucial to confront cardiovascular disease. Hence, a multimodality imaging method combing US with optical coherence tomography (OCT) is created to improve both imaging resolution and penetration [101]. Other invasive US procedures include transesophageal echocardiography (TEE), transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS), endoscopic ultrasound (EUS), transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) and intraoperative ultrasound (IOUS). TEE is an alter technique to conventional echocardiography (TTE), with improved resolution and overcome the inferiority that TTE cannot visualize cardiac structures efficiently due to the combination with US, used to overcome the acoustic obstacles present in the chest [102,103]. TVUS is a breakthrough technology in the application of US in obstetrics and gynecology in recent years, for better gynecological diagnosis and to scan the fetus during the early stages of pregnancy. The high-frequency probe (5 ∼ 7.5 MHz) is fitted with a disposable rubber sheath, and then the outer and inner layers of the sleeve are coated with a coupling and placed in the vagina, place the probe close to the cervix and posterior fornix in the vagina, and the angle of the probe is rotated and adjusted to obtain high-resolution images and results [104,105]. EUS is a gastrointestinal examination technique combining endoscopy and US, which plays a crucial role in the preoperative stage of early gastrointestinal cancer, as well as the origin and depth of gastrointestinal tumors. EUS puts a miniature high-frequency ultrasonic probe on the top of the endoscope, and can obtain ultrasonic images of the characteristics of the tissues in the stomach and intestines through ultrasonic scanning through the endoscope entering the body, so as to provide doctors with a more intuitive and accurate diagnosis of the disease and make targeted treatment plans [106]. TRUS is an US examination performed by placing an intracavitary probe inside the rectum. The imaging is acquired by observing the shape, size, position, and involvement of the rectum, prostate, bladder, and is helpful for the diagnosis of prostate hyperplasia and prostate cancer [107,108]. IOUS is used in the operation to probe directly on the surface of the organ in order to understand the hemodynamic changes and pathological changes of the organ during the operation.

Invasive US possesses its unique advantages in some special fields, but it remains many aspects to be improved. As an invasive imaging technique, safety and comfort cannot compare with that of non-invasive imaging technique, which limits its widespread application to some extent. For instance, during the examination process, EUS may lead to mucosal damage, gastrointestinal bleeding, and, in severe cases, perforation [109,110]. Nevertheless, invasive ultrasound offers enhanced resolution and more comprehensive pathological insights compared to non-invasive imaging techniques. This results in more valuable guidance, not only for scientific research but also for clinical applications [111]. What’s more, despite the tremendous improvements in non-invasive and invasive imaging techniques, it is insufficient to confront diseases suitable for US imaging, such as cardiovascular disease. While US-imaging can furnish abundant anatomical and physiological information, it exhibits limitations in capturing details related to biological metabolism and molecular activity-areas where optical imaging excels. Furthermore, in certain scenarios, PET and MRI outperform ultrasound imaging due to their reduced constraints regarding penetration depth [112,113]. PET tracers synthesized by novel 18F-labeling technologies permit the assessment of in vivo pharmacokinetics and enable accurate prediction of therapeutic dose in individual patients [114].

Currently, clinical applications demand personalized, precise, and high-resolution imaging methods, a challenge that single imaging modalities find difficult to meet. Therefore, the trend is shifting towards multimodal imaging to address these requirements. It is expected that PET-CT and PET-MRI synergetic imaging techniques can make up for the US imaging to some extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contrast agents in in vivo ultrasound imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Novel NPs-based contrast agents have propelled US imaging towards new cellular and molecular directions, improving its sensitivity and specificity in detecting earlier stages of disease, with low potential risks and sight side effect. Nanoparticles with the appropriate structure and performance that can bind to the target provide a powerful strategy for targeted imaging, and US guided therapeutic strategies. Safety considerations associated with the use of contrast agents in ultrasound imaging include:

(i) Kidney function: Impaired kidney function can increase the risk of complications when using contrast agents, as the kidneys are responsible for excreting them. (ii) Microbubble stability: Ensuring the stability of microbubble contrast agents is crucial to prevent unwanted microbubble destruction, which can affect imaging quality. (iii) Dosage control: Proper dosage control is essential to avoid overdosing, which can lead to adverse effects, and (iv) Patient screening: Screening patients for contraindications, such as allergies or kidney problems, before administering contrast agents is essential to ensure patient safety. Generally, the contrast agents employed in US are divided into three classes based on whether their core is gas, solid or liquid, which will be fully discussed in the following section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: US contrast agents with a gas core
ce:section.ce:para: US contrast agents with a gas core induce high acoustic impedance differences within tissue interfaces, and can generate the highest acoustic intensity compared to the other types of contrast agents. The utilization of gas-core US contrast agents dates back to 1980, in studies where microbubbles (MBs) were produced by agitating normal saline serum using syringes connected to a stopcock [118]. More recently nano-bubbles (NBs) were shown to enable deeper penetration, including into tumor tissues through the endothelial gap which accumulate at the target site. Both MBs and NBs have a gas core surrounded by lipid or polymer shells. Despite the strong correlation between particle size and acoustic echo reflectivity, which makes MBs attractive for US imaging, NBs have the advantage that they can be actively targeted in the extravascular space to detect cancer [119].

As depicted in Fig. 4 a, gas-filled MBs are fabricated by ultrasonic treatment on the aqueous dispersion of fluorinated copolypeptide in the presence of octafluoropropane (C3F8) [115]. A perfluorooctanoyl (PFOK) block presents low interfacial tension and Laplace pressure at the inner C3F8 phase. The stability of MBs under both long-term storage and B-mode imaging conditions can be further enhanced by UV-actuated PDAK crosslinking for PEG-PDAK-PFOK stabilized MBs. Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) imaging performance can be further enhanced by the in situ reaction of peripheral myocardial infarction moieties with albumin, which is the most abundant plasma protein, under both in vitro and in vivo conditions. Perfluorocarbon (PFC) is a valuable US contrast agent used in clinical applications for its low rate of diffusion across blood capillaries and excellent capability to improve backscattering US performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: US contrast agents with a liquid core
ce:section.ce:para: Liquid-core US contrast agents provide a relatively poor contrast enhancement because of their weak acoustic scattering inside the arteries due to their low impedance. For this reason, nanodroplets (NDs) are often converted from a liquid phase to a gas phase by heating after their delivery to the specific target. Specifically, when exposed to ultrasound, NDs vaporize from liquid nanoparticles into gas microbubbles, and can deliver their cargo with high spatial precision to the target tissue, while generating acoustic emissions [120].

As shown in Fig. 4b, PFC droplets (perfluoropentane, PFP or perfluorohexane, PFH) could be encapsulated into PDA by hydrophobic interaction, and PEG-NH2 can be used to modify PFC-PDA nanoparticles to improve their physiological stability [116]. PFC-PDA-PEG can translate light into heat to kill tumor cells under near-infrared (NIR) laser irradiation, and PFP (PFH) droplets can transform from liquid to gas phase for US imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: US contrast agents with a solid core
ce:section.ce:para: Solid-core contrast agents have specific properties that can be developed for US contrast enhancement. These properties include higher stability and desirable acoustic impedance changes between the solid-core materials and soft tissue. These features of solid-core US contrast agents lead to more robust reflectivity production, an improved signal to noise ratio (SNR), and greater contrast enhancement. They present high intravascular and soft tissue echo intensities [121,122]. These NPs are advantageous in terms of their small size, multi-functionality, favorable stability, facile fabrication process, highly targeted turnover, high abundance, and versatile structures. Typically, solid-based ultrasound contrast agents have a relatively lower acoustic intensity than organic types; e.g., MBs. Solid-core agents are made up of different materials, including silica, gold, carbon, magnetic, which are reviewed below.

A good example of a solid core/shell US contrast agent is the fabrication of gold-nanoshelled microcapsules (Fig. 4c) [117]. The figure shows polymeric microcapsules that were generated from PLA and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) materials by employing the water-in-oil-in-water (W/O/W) double-emulsion method, as follows: I) electrostatic adsorption of PAH onto microcapsules; Ⅱ) deposition of gold nanoparticles onto PAH-coated microcapsules; Ⅲ) formation of gold nanoshells by the surface-seeding method; Ⅳ) lyophilization to sublimate the encapsulated water in the inner aqueous phase of the microcapsules to produce small hollow spaces to provide a basis for the ultrasound-responsive properties. The pulse-inversed harmonic imaging (PIHI) mode and conventional B-mode exhibited strong properties after injecting the ultrasound contrast of gold-nanoshelled microcapsules.

Briefly, owing to the low rate of diffusion across blood capillaries and excellent capability to improve backscattering US performance, the PFC gas-based US contrast agent is more popularized in the clinical applications, while liquid-based US contrast agent presents excellent stability [123–125]. Solid-based US contrast agents hold great promise in the multifunctional diagnosis and treatment of diseases. Their inherent mechanical strength provides stability, enabling them to effectively load hydrophilic, hydrophobic, or both types of species. Additionally, their ease of functionalization is attributed to the presence of functional groups, further enhancing their versatility in biomedical applications [126,127]. Despite significant advancements, there is still a considerable distance to cover before achieving clinical translation. For instance, challenges persist in extending the imaging time window of US contrast agents, and efforts are needed to enhance their stability, thereby mitigating the decay of US signals.

Advances in US technologies have led to user-programmable systems that can generate a variety of transmitted pulse trains, and schemes for image reconstruction. Despite these advances, US still suffers from limitations in resolution, contrast, signal to noise ratio (SNR), and artifacts. While optical super-resolution microscopy techniques have revolutionized the observation of living structures at the cellular scale, offering an improved spatial resolution as compared to the traditional diffraction limit, there has been no breakthrough in US imaging [128,129].Though traditional US techniques, such as US deconvolution [130,131], monostatic synthetic aperture [132], and constrained least squares beamforming [133], succeed in improving spatial resolution, these methods have drawbacks for real-time medical applications, including the enhancement of both the signal and noise, which reduces the SNR(Signal to Noise Ratio), the introduction of artifacts, and poor contrast resolution due to higher side lobes. Recently, a new technology to implement a structured illumination mode for US imaging has been suggested, with the specific goal of improving spatial resolution [134], in which the imaging can be generated and captured by the transducer and result from the acoustic wavefront manipulation. All it takes is controlling the phase and apodization of each individual transducer element, endowing the method with the capacities of being dynamic and reconfigured in real-time. The structured illumination mode for US imaging involves shifting the emitted pattern between subsequent pulses and then capturing images. This necessitates the calculation of all phase and apodization maps before the imaging session. The emitted field can be dynamically regulated to generate shifted patterns, allowing for image acquisition and subsequent reconstruction of a super-resolution image. The development of methods for super-resolution US imaging, in addition to currently available optical structured illumination microscopy (SIM), is a significant step forward since optical microscopy does not penetrate tissue well, whereas US systems are designed to image deep organs due to the high tissue penetration of US. For many clinical applications, super resolution US is highly effective when needing to resolve structures with sub-diffraction dimensions. Synthetic aperture US imaging (SAI), is an advanced medical imaging technology that synthesizes multiple small-aperture US imaging data to form a large-aperture image, resulting in higher resolution and clearer images. The principle of SAI is to use US to send and receive the returned echo signals at different positions, and to form an image with a larger effective aperture through calculation and combination of these echo signals, so as to improve the image clarity [135]. Plane wave imaging is an ultra-fast ultrasonic imaging method, using parallel processing of phased array scanning mode, adopting one unfocused beam emission corresponding to multiple ultrasonic beam reception and relying on the echo signal transmitted to image the specific area [136]. Microbubble-based imaging is the most common imaging of US imaging. It allows the indirect observation of vessels through intravascular microbubbles. The injected bubbles of air or perfluorocarbon increase the intensity of blood vessels by more than a factor of a hundred even in small concentrations (in the order of 1 per 105 red blood cells) due to impedance mismatch between blood and gas [137,138]. Ultrasound localization microscopy (ULM) is an emerging technique providing impressive super-resolved images of microvasculature, i.e. Images acquired by ULM possess much better resolution than the traditional diffraction-limited US techniques and have achieved preliminary transformation from preclinical to clinical application [139].

While US imaging in the medicine has been increasing in popularity over the years, US imaging presents some limitations that in some cases are difficult to overcome, mostly associated with the imaging modality. On one hand, US imaging is interfered with noise and visual artifacts due to the intrinsic characteristics of the emitted/received signal, coming with low contrast. This defect requires the operator to be highly skilled [140]. On the other hand, human breathing is unavoidable and this respiratory motion can cause a serious data quality reduction during the US imaging, which is common in thoracic or abdominal organs, e.g., liver, heart, or lungs [141]. Finally, limited by US sensor, US imaging can only present a small region of the human body. Hence, it must be required to be combined with other imaging techniques (such as CT or MRI) [142].

Ultrasonic-based energy conversion nanomedicine [143] has shown great potential in cancer therapy due to its satisfactory therapeutic performance, low permeability limit, and non-invasive nature. Studies indicate that cavitation, the mechanical and thermal effects generated during ultrasound therapy, may lead to the formation of cytotoxic reactive oxygen species [144,145]. On the one hand, the focused ultrasound (FUS) beam can be absorbed by the tissue and converted into thermal energy, which induces a rapid rise in temperature and generates a large amount of heat for thermal ablation of tumors [146]. On the other, the deposited ultrasound energy produces a cavitation effect, which manifests in the appearance, rapid growth and collapse of microbubbles that cause considerable mechanical damage [147] to the tumor tissue due to its inherent mechanical wave properties.

Brain science is pivotal in the realm of human life research. However, the presence of the blood–brain barrier (BBB) imposes limitations on drug delivery while serving as a protective barrier for the brain. In recent years, there have been reports indicating that US can temporarily open the BBB, sparking inspiration among many scientists. Furthermore, as a relatively new modality for neuromodulation, US demonstrates a robust and safe influence on neural tissue, impacting neural activity both positively and negatively, especially in the brain. This has been substantiated by a growing body of research. The mechanisms through which US waves regulate cellular and molecular processes in neural tissue remain unknown. This is partly due to the intricate nature of US interactions with tissue, involving mechanical force, heating, and cavitation. However, this complexity has not diminished its popularity. Noteworthy achievements, among others, include US stimulation in nonhuman primates. For instance, sonication has been demonstrated to modulate cognitive behavior in monkeys. Additionally, in human studies, US has been utilized to modulate the primary somatosensory cortex, resulting in the attenuation of the amplitudes of somatosensory evoked potentials [148–150]. US-based minimally invasive therapy plays a significant role in clinical translational medicine and application, especially in the ultrasound assisted research in brain science. The activation effects of US on frog muscles are observed in 1940, leading neuroscientists to investigate the effects of US on the nervous system [151,152]. US has been reported to modulate the neural activity in the thalamus, cortex, and hippocampus of different species, including humans [153–155]. US is regarded as a non-invasive approach to modulating neural activity precisely due to its penetrating and focusable characteristics.

US excites neurons via biophysical effect since it can produce multiple physical phenomena in tissue, including raising temperature, inducing bubble formation and cavitation, and applying mechanical force (Fig. 5 a), each of which can cause neuronal excitation [156]. Optogenetics, a emerging technique combining genetic engineering with light provides a temporally precise and neuron-type-specific dissection of complex neural circuits and brain functions, and as such as revolutionized neuroscience research. The challenge related to the invasive delivery of a light source is the limited tissue penetration of photons for optogenetic stimulation in the brain. Despite considerable efforts in this field, including applying opsins with red-shifted activation spectra and 2-photon stimulation to enable optogenetic neural modulation in the intact brain of live animals without implants, and intracranially injecting up-conversion nanoparticles which absorb brain-penetrant near-infrared (NIR) light and emit visible light into the mouse brain for deep brain optogenetic stimulation by delivering NIR irradiation from an optical fiber placed outside the skull, these methods have significant limitations, such as requiring partial removal of the scalp and skull to afford deeper penetration. Focused ultrasound (FUS) can address these challenges of optogenetics because it can penetrate more deeply into biological tissues including the brain. Specific nanoparticles can act as rechargeable light sources in blood circulation for sono-optogenetics via US (Fig. 5b) [157]. Fig. 5c presents the mechanism governing US-triggered light emission from these specific nanoparticles. Electron traps are generated by doping ions in the main material, causing the photoexcited electrons to be trapped after absorption of 400 nm excitation light. FUS allows the trapped electrons to transfer energy into the luminescent centers created by the dopant ions, resulting in 470-nm light emission. The specific nanoparticles in blood circulation can transport the 400-nm photoexcitation energy via the superficial vessels into a 470-nm emission in deep-brain regions for optogenetic stimulation (Fig. 5d) and open ChR2 channels via conversion to 470-nm light emission by nanoparticles when triggered by US (Fig. 5e). FUS may be able to breach the BBB, thus overcoming one of the major hurdles to the development of innovative drugs in neuroscience. This relies on the stable cavitation of circulating microbubbles to mechanically open the tight junctions forming the BBB. This opening allows for the permeation of particles up to tens of nm in size, including small molecules, nanoparticles, drug and viral vectors, combined with targeted neurons (Fig. 5f) [158]. In acoustically targeted chemogenetics (ATAC), the FUS-BBB opening is used to transduce neurons at specific locations in the brain with virally encoded engineered chemogenetic receptors. These receptors can then respond to systemically administered designer compounds to activate or inhibit the activity of these neurons as needed for experimental perturbation or treatment (Fig. 5g).

Although promising, ultrasonic neuromodulation is still in the early stages of development, with open questions remaining with regard to expression and tolerability of relevant material in the brain and their molecular sensitivity. Another important question concerning ultrasonic neuromodulation is its specificity and potential side effects. Some studies in small animals show that motor responses cannot follow the expected spatial targeting of US. Ye et al. reports that stimulating only one side of the motor cortex allows for the actuation of bilateral body movements [159]. In addition, while ultrasonic neuromodulation can be facilitated by the design of improved and simplified methods to target US to special brain regions with steerable transducer arrays,[160,161] the modulatory effects observed are relatively modest and divergent in terms of their polarity and duration, proving a long road before achieving excellent ultrasonic neuromodulation.

Thermal therapy is a wide range of treatments for diseases due to the fact that temperature plays a crucial role in the body, and hyperthermia can be induced by stimuli such as light, magnetic field, US, etc, whose intensity is in direct proportion to the increase in temperature to some extent [164]. As a means of sono-thermal therapy, high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) has attracted much attention from researchers. HIFU is generally considered a non-invasive extracorporeal ablation technology that focuses high-intensity US energy to ablate and destroy tumor cells/tissues. Compared to normal tissues, the solid tumor microenvironment has a distinct composition [165] characterized by high cell density, high vascular epidermal permeability, high interstitial pressure, an abnormal extracellular matrix, and a lack of structural support to drain lymph [166,167]. US-enhanced drug delivery has been reported in a range of disease models. For example, FUS can temporarily increase BBB permeability [168–170]. but requires innovative methods to deliver a sufficient amount of drug from the blood vessels to the tumor interior. Drug penetration properties can be improved several fold when mediated by US [171]. However, some drugs are unstable in physiological environments, which reduces the efficiency of US-mediated drug delivery. However, by encapsulating the naked drug into the nano-carrier and controlling the release of the loaded drug through US stimulation, the release of the drug in the lesion site can be enhanced and the potential toxic and side effects on other normal tissues avoided [172].

Real-time US imaging guidance makes the HIFU treatment process easy to monitor [173]. Compared to the typical cancer-treatment protocols, HIFU has distinct advantages including non-invasive operations, small focus, non-radioactivity and no damage to normal tissues in the acoustic-propagating path. HIFU has been extensively applied clinically to treat several kinds of primary solid tumors and even metastatic cancer. However, the delivery of therapy to deep, larger volumes and abundant blood-supplied tissues is unsatisfactory due to the decrease in US energy in areas that are adjacent to high-speed blood flow. While a simple increase of US energy could address this challenge, it would likely cause damage to normal tissue. What’s more, there are some challenges remaining to be overcame, such as the limited penetration of high-intensity US, inefficient distribution, clearance and metabolism of relevent nanoparticles. The feasible solutions include targeted drug delivery [174–176], enhancement of permeation and retention [177], combination therapies [178,179], etc. Recently, microbubble US contrast agents can synergistically enhance HIFU therapeutic efficiency. Fig. 6 a presents a biocompatible multifunctional nanosystem with phase-changing features for trans-arterial chemoembolization-enhanced sono-thermal cancer surgery [162]. A typical double emulsion process was employed to concurrently embed Fe3O4 nanoparticles into the shell of poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) and liquid perfluorohexane (PFH) into the core of PLGA nanocapsules. Magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles can act as contrast agents for T2-weighted MRI and PA imaging, providing imaging guidance. The combination of Fe3O4-PFH/PLGA and Lipiodal can transiently block blood flow and prolong/ increase the accumulation of composite nanocapsules within the tumor area. The subsequent PFH bubble generation from Fe3O4-PFH/PLGA via HIFU irradiation further enhances the cavitation effect and simultaneously promotes micro-vessel occlusion of the tumor. Unlike synergistic agents (SAs) based on biocompatible micro-/nanoparticles that can change the acoustic environment of tissues, increase the ultrasonic cavitation and enhance the energy deposition to efficiently enhance HIFU surgical efficacy, traditional SAs are too large to infiltrate into the tumor tissues and their biocompatibility and degradation for further clinical translations are controversial. Recently, a “small-to-big” phase-transformation strategy was developed to address this problem. The liquid core of synthesized nano- emulsions is PFH, whose hydrophilic shells consist of biocompatible phosphatidylcholine, DSPE-PEG(2000)-folate, glycerol phospholipids, cholesterol and pulronic poloxame (Fig. 6b) [163]. These tailored targeted nanoemulsions (TNEs) are highly temperature-sensitive and can be vaporized upon exposure to high temperatures and/or HIFU irradiation. The vaporization makes nano-emulsions generate large amounts of micrometer-sized bubbles via the phase transformation from liquid to gas, thus improving the US imaging and enhancing HIFU’s therapeutic efficiency.

As an emerging non-invasive treatment, sonodynamic therapy (SDT) uses sonosensitizers to generate cytotoxic ROS through low-intensity US excitation, which causes the apoptosis/necrosis of cancer cells [182–184]. At present, the mechanism of SDT is not conclusive, but there are two mainstream views that are sonoluminescence and pyrolysis. Sonoluminescence means that US blasts the liquid in the body to produce bubbles, and produces light when the gas bubble bursts, which acts on the acoustic sensitizer and converts it into an excited state. Pyrolysis refers to the use of local high temperature and high pressure in the acoustic dynamic process to activate the sonosensitizer. Regardless of the mechanism, ROS is currently recognized as the primary effector of SDT [185,186]. Reactive free radicals can be generated via the sonosensitizer-based method and thermolysis of water (Fig. 7 a) [55]. In the former, the energy of US is released and sensed by a proximal sonosensitizer based on the acoustic cavitation effect, which activates oxygen to generate reactive oxygen species. Thermolysis of water means that the local hotspot can also lysis the surrounding water molecules to generate ·OH and ·H. Due to the poor tissue penetration of NIR light into deep tumors, conventional photodynamic therapy (PDT) is usually limited to treating superficial tumors [187,188]. Compared to the NIR laser, US has low tissue attenuation, high precision, low invasiveness and good biocompatibility, and can penetrate deeper into biological tissues with less energy attenuation and activate sonosensitizers with higher spatial accuracy. The most representative sonosensitizers include inorganic sonosensitizers such as TiO2 NPs [189,190] and organic sonosensitizers such as hematoporphyrin (HP)[191–194], protoporphyrin IX [195–197], phthalocyanine [198], etc., which have been extensively developed for cancer SDT. However, traditional organic sonosensitizers usually have some unavoidable obstacles such as poor water solubility, rapid clearance from the body and short blood circulation time [199,200]. This results in insufficient accumulation of ideal locations, thus reducing their SDT efficiency. Coupling organic sonosensitizers to the surface of nanoparticles may provide an alternative strategy to improve the efficacy of free organic sonosensitizers SDT [183,201–203]. In addition, SDT has been shown to increase the susceptibility of cancer cells to chemotherapy drugs by accelerating the accumulation of drugs at the tumor site. Fig. 7b presents the design/fabrication of a high-performance multifunctional nanoparticulate sonosensitizer for efficient in-vivo magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-guided SDT against cancer [180]. Hollow mesoporous organo-silica nanoparticles (HMONs) of molecularly organic–inorganic hybridized framework contribute to the anchoring of sonosensitizers for the imaging-guided SDT. The large surface area of HMONs provides sufficient amounts of anchoring sites for covalently grafting small organic sonosensitizers into the mesopores with loading amounts. When the sonosensitizers are irradiated by US, they can produce ROS by SDT and diffuse to the surrounding environment to deliver treatment. The paramagnetic Mn centers in MnPpIX dispersed within the mesopores facilitate direct interaction with the hydrogen protons of water molecules, thus improving T1-weighted MRI performance. Progress in SDT has improved the performance of sonosensitizers. Specific sonosensitizers can enhance SDT action against hypoxic tumors by photoetching oxygen vacancies on the surface (Fig. 7c) [181]. Specific sonosensitizers have unique band structures that can catalyze the decomposition of H2O to generate O2 and ROS in situ by coordinating with enriched oxygen vacancies that extend the lifetime of sono-excited charge carriers and also enhance the production of O2 and ROS, thus considerably mitigating tumor hypoxia.

Although SDT promising, there are still some issues to be solved before the clinical translation of SDT [204]. Firstly, the biosafety, biocompatibility, biodegradability, and therapeutic efficacy of sonosensitizers need to be improved. Then, the real mechanism underlying SDT is one of the primary focuses of current research and additional in-depth research is still required. What’s more, US irradiation optimization is urgently required to be further explored because the influence factors of cavitation effect, such as ultrasonic intensity and frequency, treatment time of US, pulse number, and period, etc., put a great influence on the effect of SDT. These crucial issues are stumbling blocks to the clinical transformation of SDT. Addressing these issues is of decisive influence for the development of SDT.

There are few studies on the clinical applications of SDT given its lack of high biocompatibility or satisfactory therapeutic effects, although remarkable achievements and advances have been made. Crucially, the metabolism of sonosensitizers in vivo needs to be evaluated comprehensively to ensure their long-term biological safety. Further, the stability of sonosensitizers is key to improving the therapeutic effect and reducing off-target toxicity. Simple design, large-batch and repeatable production are integral to promoting clinical translation. However, the underlying mechanism as to how SDT inhibits tumor growth remains a mystery. The study of its specific mechanism will guide the design of sonosensitizers in the future.

Previous studies have shown that monotherapy does not eliminate all tumor cells. Therefore, a variety of combination therapies such as SDT/Autophagy, SDT/ Ferroptosis, SDT/photothermal therapy (PTT), SDT/chemodynamic therapy (CDT), SDT/ immunogenic cell death (ICD) and others can achieve better results. Table 1 lists these applications.

As depicted in Fig. 8 a, a high-efficiency and safe sonosensitizer for photothermal-enhanced SDT were reported [219]. The sonosensitizer promotes its endocytosis and improved the efficacy of SDT after photothermal treatment under laser irradiation. The oxygen-defective Ti3C2 NPs (H-Ti3C2 NPs) are synthetized by two-step methods of chemical exfoliation and high-temperature treatment. H-Ti3C2 NPs present great SDT efficacy than Ti3C2 NPs for that the oxygen defect structure of H-Ti3C2 NPs can prevent the recombination of e--h+ pairs, which enhances the SDT effect under US irradiation. H-Ti3C2 NPs also act as a photothermal agent due to high absorbance in the NIR Ⅱ window, which could produce heat under laser irradiation. This strategy of PTT-enhanced SDT synergetic therapy highlights the extension of the biomedical application of Mxene nanoplatforms. Although TiO2 is a typical sonosensitizer, its SDT performance alone is not sufficient for tumor treatment. However, titanium monoxide (TiO1+x) particles with an ultrafine nanorod (NR) structure can be synthetized via a seed-mediated growth mechanism into a new type of SDT agent (Fig. 8b) [220]. Compared to traditional TiO2 sonosensitizers, TiO1+x NRs have higher US-triggered ROS generation efficiency due to the oxygen-deficient structure of TiO1+x, which provides charge traps to limit the recombination of US-triggered election-hole pairs. In addition, TiO1+x NRs also possess horseradish-peroxidase-like nanozyme activity which can catalytic hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to produce hydroxyl radicals (·OH), serving as a CDT effect, and eliminate tumors more efficiently. SDT can also be combined with autophagy to confront tumors (Fig. 8c) [221]. CCP@HP@M is constructed by doping hollow polydopamine nanoparticles with Pt nanoparticles, which is modified with the cell membrane, as well as loaded with sonosensitizer Ce6 and the autophagy inhibitor CQ. The nanoparticles are delivered to tumor tissues by intravenous injection, and release sonosensitizer Ce6, autophagy inhibitor CQ, and Pt nanoparticles under the inducement of US and acidic tumor microenvironment to enhance ROS and block autophagy in SDT for colorectal cancer. HP not only serves as a carrier but also mimics SOD-like activity to elevate the level of H2O2. Pt nanozymes acts as a CAT-like catalyst, decomposing endogenous H2O2 to generate O2 in situ, resulting in hypoxia alleviation and SDT enhancement. In addition, the POD-like activity of Pt nanozymes catalyzes ·OH production, thus contributing to cancer therapy. CCP@HP@M releases the loaded Ce6 and chloroquine under acidic conditions. The released Ce6 is further released in response to SDT under US irradiation and the released CQ can block autophagic flux, and reduce the resistance of colon cancer to SDT, thus achieving the SDT/CDT treatment effect. SDT can also be combined with ferroptosis to achieve synergistic therapeutic effects (Fig. 8d) [222]. The Aza-boron-dipyrromethene (AzaBDY) dye-based therapeutic nanoplatform is constructed by the self-assembly of Aza-BDY dye bearing an acrylate moiety and the biocompatible polymer, 1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-[methoxy(polyethylene glycol)-2000] (DSPE-PEG2000). After being internalized by the tumor cells, Aza-BDY NPs can selectively deplete intracellular Cys due to the Michael addition of Cys with the acrylate group of AzaBDY and the subsequent cyclization reaction which disrupts the intracellular redox balance and promotes ferroptotic cell death. The released sonosensitizer (Azb-BDY) can produce appreciable amounts of O2 under the US irradiation to achieve the SDT effect.

Immunotherapy is of great interest as an emerging and powerful cancer therapy that aims to mobilize the host immune system against tumor cells at both the primary and distal sites, and can also induce strong immune protection against future tumorigenesis. Immunotherapy has the advantages of strong specificity, low toxicity, prevention of tumor metastasis and recurrence, and has great a potential to become a mainstream cancer treatment strategy. Immunotherapy is revolutionizing cancer treatment, which has shown some clinical efficacy [223].

As mentioned above, US produce ROS, ablation, and high pressure, which can lead to tumor cell death. Accumulating evidence has confirmed that SDT can induce immunogenic cell death (ICD) and trigger a systemic anti-tumor immune response, manifesting high performance for sonodynamic immunotherapy [96,97]. Currently, numerous innovative SDT have emerged as effective treatments for cancer. These therapies have the potential to not only target and eliminate large primary tumors but also address metastatic tumors. Traditional cancer treatments like chemotherapy often come with side effects, including pain and other adverse reactions. Sonodynamic immunotherapy shows promise in mitigating these side effects associated with conventional cancer therapies.

Fig. 9 depicts examples of recent SDT assisted ICD therapy, and illustrates the rapid development of sonodynamic immunotherapy. For example, porphyrin COF showed enhanced sonodynamic performance after being coordinated by Fe3+ and coated with PLG-g-mPEG due to the regularity of the spatial arrangement of porphyrin molecules and the improvement of chemical structure stability (Fig. 9a) [224]. This sonosensitizer also has CDT performance based on the Fenton reaction and the enhanced tumor killing ability of glutathione (GSH) consumption. When combined with the α-PD-L1 antibody, this sonosensitizer can lead to highly effective tumor immunotherapy. Many preclinical research trials have indicated that the fragment from tumor cells induced by SDT may serve as a source of tumor antigens to set up host anti-tumor immunological effects. Unfortunately, this immunological effect induced by SDT is too weak to efficiently inhibit tumor growth and metastasis. However, this discovery is not meaningless. Sonodynamic immunotherapy exhibits higher tumor treatment efficiency than immunotherapy alone. Fig. 9b presents a good example of sonodynamic immunotherapy [225]. The tumor-associated antigens in situ derived from the SDT of the primary tumors possess vaccine-like functions comparable to an immune adjuvant, enabling the sonosensitizer to elicit an immune response by stimulating dendritic cell (DCs) maturation and cytokine secretion which thus can kill tumor cells. After coordinating with an anti-PD-L1 checkpoint blockade, the systematic antitumor immune responses increase. This sonodynamic immunotherapy strategy also provides a long-term immunological memory function, protecting against tumor rechallenge after elimination of the initial tumors. Apart from traditional sonosensitizers, sono-immunotherapeutic nanobodies (SPNab) have excellent sonodynamic properties and applications for SDT, which are applied in PTT for their good optical properties, high photostability and excellent biocompatibility (Fig. 9c) [226]. The amphiphilic polymer conjugates consist of a semiconducting polymer backbone, a poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG) chain and an 1O2-cleavable linker. The semiconducting polymer backbone is responsible for self-assembly to form the main body of the SPNab. The PEG chain improves the biocompatibility and sites combining with the 1O2-cleavable linker. The 1O2-cleavable linker is integrated with the anti-CTLA4 antibody for the ICD effect. After the US irradiation, SPNab generates 1O2 not only to induce ICD for enhanced tumor immunogenicity, but also to activate the 1O2-cleavable linker to remotely release anti-CTLA4 antibodies to trigger a checkpoint blockade in situ. This sonodynamic immunotherapy mediated by SPNab regulates the tumoricidal function of T-cell immunity by promoting the proliferation of cytotoxic T lymphocytes and depleting immunosuppressive regulatory T cells, eradicating tumors, inhibiting metastasis and protecting against tumor relapse. In fact, the structure of SPNab is so flexible that it allows considerable generalization of the sonodynamic activation approach to modulate other immunotherapeutic pathways. Fig. 9d presents another sonodynamic immunotherapy mediated by SPNab that utilizes a different semiconducting polymer backbone and other ligands/antibodies [218]. Similarly, under the irradiation of ultrasound, SPNab exhibits many tumor-targeted effects: (i) it debulks tumor tissues, (ii) scissors the 1O2-cleavable linkers to remotely release the immunomodulators, NLG919 and anti-PD-L1 antibody (aPDL1) and (iii) reprograms the tumor microenvironment through upregulating the expression levels of PD-L1 and indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase (IDO). NLG919 not only promotes the proliferation of cytotoxic T lymphocytes, but also inhibits regulatory T-cells. The aPD-L1 inhibits T-cell exhaustion and promotes the tumoricidal functions of T-cell immunity by combining with PD-L1 on the surface of PD-L1+ T cells. While this sonodynamic immunotherapy reduces the probability of increased risk of immune-related adverse events, it elicits high antitumor immunity to reject both primary and metastatic distant tumors and prevent tumor recurrence.

Sonodynamic immunotherapy still faces challenges. Immunotherapeutic agents are usually injected systemically into the body and have limited accumulation in tumors, leading to a poor therapeutic response and immune-related adverse events. The development of nano sonosensitizers can accurately deliver immune therapeutic agents, which should be able to solve these problems satisfactorily. In this section, we briefly describe several common combination therapies that can significantly inhibit tumor growth and distant metastasis, and even prevent tumor recurrence. This work provides a new idea for tumor treatment. However, the distribution and potential toxicity of the material in the body are unknown, and remain a major problem. Thus, combination therapy might be more effective and have fewer disadvantages than any single treatment. The real-time monitoring of immune response and the generation of ROS after treatment is crucial to develop a standard for comprehensive evaluation of SDT related characteristics. The target area and the edges of complex tumors also require attention; hence, various imaging techniques need to be used beforehand. Although there will be many hurdles to the development of more advanced sonodynamic immunotherapy, time will show that this treatment is not only suitable for laboratory research, but can one day be applied in the clinic to benefit patients, but in any case, nanomaterials help leading to significant breakthroughs.

Non-invasive diagnostic and treatment approaches with high spatiotemporal resolution are essential in advancing modern medicine. Among these approaches, US stands out as a non-invasive, real-time, and cost-effective clinical imaging modality, playing a significant role in chemical synthesis and clinical translation, particularly in the realms of in vivo imaging and cancer therapy. This review provides a comprehensive overview of recent developments in ultrasound-assisted sonochemical synthesis and its potential biological applications. While these applications hold great promise, there are still areas in need of improvement. Our review is divided into three main sections: ultrasound-assisted synthesis, ultrasound contrast agents, and ultrasound-assisted therapy.

I. US assisted synthesis: Sonochemical synthesis has been widely developed over the past few decades. Compared to coprecipitation, sol–gel, and solvothermal methods, the sonochemical synthesis strategy manifests high efficiency for the fabrication of typical nanoparticles. However, the sonochemical synthesis method is still limited by its poor control of the size, morphology, and composition [227,228]. Another challenge is scale-up synthesis and energy conversion efficiency. Specifically, the coupling of US into chemically based cavitation events remains a low-yield event, which calls for further investigation [229,230].

II. US contrast agents: Despite its major advantages, there is still a real need to improve US diagnostic resolution due to the difficulty of confirming small organs in dense tissues. Further efforts are required to improve the number of mapped tissue properties and their ability to localize. Traditional US lacks soft tissue contrast and is user- dependent. Super resolution methods, as well as the increased use of contrast agents in US, have dramatically improved US capabilities for in-vivo imaging, and suggest this may constitute major route to improve US imaging capabilities. As regards US contrast agents, there are still some fundamental challenges to their optimal design. One controversial issue in preparing US contrast agents is in their size design and control in the preparation steps [231–233]. In addition, their size can change during circulation through the vascular system, since they interact with plasma proteins, which can lead to a change in the surface charge of the agents, giving rise to larger sizes. This size modification is amplified when the US contrast materials are gas- based with a larger size, since their circulation is faster thus reducing their ability to target tumors. Another obstacle is that some of the microbubbles are destroyed over time by the high ultrasound acoustic pressure. As a result, solid-based ultrasound contrast agents tend to be ductile materials with favorable degradability and greater resistance to acoustic output compared to bubbles, suggesting an alternative approach to gas-based US contrast agents.

Despite these challenges in US imaging, advances in fabrication and the design of transducers, the development of novel image processing techniques, and a fundamental change in pulse sequences, US is a promising imaging tool, with the potential of cost effective, non -radiative and high accuracy imaging and early-stage disease detection.

III. US assisted therapy: To date, US assisted synergetic therapy, especially for combination strategies with ICD, has proved to be a strong candidate for clinical translational medicine applications for the diagnosis and treatment of cancer, given its satisfactory therapeutic performance, low permeability limit, and non-invasive nature. However, there are hurdles to overcome. In terms of SDT, traditional sonosensitizers (e.g., porphyrins and their derivatives, phthalocyanines, TiO2 etc.) are still limited by their weak targeting and release efficiency to tumor cells. Although US based synergetic therapy is efficient against tumors, the damage to normal cell is also non-negligible. The discovery and development of more targeted and safer SDT-based immunotherapy strategies are a basic prerequisite for its clinical translation. Finally, the optimization of US parameters and scale manufacturing of nanomedicines need to bear in mind that US parameters influence the efficacy of SDT, further affecting US assisted synergetic therapy, and that large-scale and reproducible synthesis is more difficult when the nanoparticle consists of multiple materials and steps.

Generally, US-assisted synthesis may not be suitable for all chemical reactions, and the optimization of US parameters can be time-consuming. The mechanical forces generated during sonication can also lead to unwanted side reactions or product degradation.

Therefore, ensuring reproducibility and scalability of US-assisted synthesis on an industrial scale can be challenging. Additionally, understanding the complex interactions between US and chemical reactions is an ongoing research area. In addition, US contrast agents may have limited stability in vivo, potentially affecting the accuracy of imaging. The need for specific formulations and handling can also be a limitation. Additionally, contrast agents may not be suitable for all imaging applications or may have safety concerns. Developing contrast agents with improved stability, biocompatibility, and targeting capabilities remains a challenge. Ensuring regulatory approval and clinical adoption of new contrast agents can also be a lengthy process. Current challenges in US-assisted therapies include depth limitations in tissue penetration, limiting their effectiveness in deep-seated tumors or tissues. Temperature control during therapy can also be challenging. In all these areas, interdisciplinary collaboration and ongoing research are essential to address limitations and overcome challenges, ultimately advancing the field of US technology in various applications.

In summary, US, as a safe, non-invasive, real-time, and cost-effective technology, plays a significant role in material synthesis, clinical translation, and other research fields, regardless of synthesis, in vivo imaging or type of cancer therapy. US assisted synergetic therapy has great research value and clinical application prospects, and is expected to become a strong alternative to traditional cancer therapies. Given the continuous rapid development of nanomedicine, US assisted synergetic therapy is likely to become our most powerful tool against tumors in the future.
Haorong Jiao: Writing – original draft. Qiulian Mao: Writing – original draft. Noman Razzaq: Writing – review & editing. Rinat Ankri: Writing – review & editing. Jiabin Cui: Supervision.
